comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a physics simulation demo involving soft-body shapes, with users noting instability, glitches, and implementation details. Some users report bugs on specific browsers and question the physics solver's robustness.

comments.author: jaen
comments.text: What's the physics solver used here? This demonstration quickly runs into common problems associated with overly simplistic solvers/integrators where everything starts to become unstable and "vibrate" / "twitch".
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies common issues with simple physics solvers causing instability, which aligns with observed behavior in the demo.

comments.author: jp57
comments.text: Because there are no instructions at all about what will happen when you click, I almost immediately created a "transporter accident" where I spawned in a new shape that intersected with an existing shape.  They became locked together, and this new compound shape seems to exist as some kind of physics engine edge case.  It struggled madly to find a low-energy state, rolling and jittering around, sometimes seeming to almost stop, and then gain energy again from nowhere and start moving again.
comments.analysis: The user provides a detailed description of an edge case in the physics simulation causing unexpected behavior, which is plausible given the demo's complexity.

comments.author: Tepix
comments.text: It seems to be broken on Firefox. It shows 3/8 players, the circles are flickering madly and I can't drag them.
comments.analysis: This comment reports a browser-specific bug, which is a reasonable observation but less about the physics accuracy and more about implementation or compatibility.

comments.author: coldcity_again
comments.text:
Bit twitchy indeed but still fun. Instantly took me back to "rubber glenz vectors" in classic Amiga prods[1]

[1] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OZTnR3FpUEA&start=413
comments.analysis: This comment is more of a nostalgic and subjective reaction to the demo's behavior rather than a technical critique.

comments.author: NewKrok
comments.text: Wow, didn't expect to see my implementation here :)
comments.analysis: This comment is a brief personal reaction without technical content or critique, adding little analytical value.
